name: foeniculum vulgare
intensity: No direct intensity data. Contextual inference: Carminative, galactagogue, anti-inflammatory. Herbs often act via neurotransmitter modulation, antioxidant effects, or enzyme inhibition.. mechanism: Mechanistic understanding is largely inferred from volatile constituents and smooth-muscle/spasmolytic pharmacology literature. EMA indications are traditional-use based, so efficacy claims should remain conservative and indication-specific.
safetyNotes: Avoid in people with hypersensitivity to fennel/anethole or other Apiaceae plants, and in mugwort-pollen cross-reactivity risk. EMA notes pregnancy/lactation safety is not established and does not recommend use in these periods without clinical supervision.

sources.title: EMA HMPC monograph (Revision 1): Foeniculum vulgare subsp. vulgare var. dulce, fructus
Kew POWO: Foeniculum vulgare (accepted name and distribution)

description: Foeniculum vulgare is an aromatic Apiaceae herb used as food and medicine. Herbal medicinal use generally relies on the dried fruit in infusion form, with traditional-use evidence rather than broad modern efficacy claims.
dosage: EMA traditional posology (adults/adolescents): 1.5 g dry fruit in 250 mL boiling water, 3 times daily (daily 4.5 g). Not for >2 weeks without clinical reassessment; pediatric use has age-specific restrictions.
interactions: EMA monograph reports no interactions for traditional sweet fennel fruit tea preparations.
preparation: EMA monograph describes sweet fennel dry fruit as herbal tea (infusion, steep ~15 minutes).
region: Native range spans parts of Macaronesia, North Africa, and Europe through western/central Asia; fennel is now widely cultivated and naturalized.
summary: Foeniculum vulgare (fennel) fruit is used traditionally for mild spasmodic gastrointestinal complaints (including bloating/flatulence), minor menstrual spasm, and cough associated with colds per EMA herbal monograph.
